Of 5000 people, while the modern
little city of Kennewick boasts of
1000 inhabitants. The future per-
manency of the district is assured
by a perpetual water right agree
ment entered into by the United
States reclamation service and the
Northern Pacific Irrigation Co. The
agreement guarantees flow of 175
cubic feet per second for all time
to come. Already 10000 acres have
been brought under ditch, and plans
are now maturing to water an addi-
tional $500 acres.


Having an elevation above sea
level of $50 feet, the Kennewick
district enjoys warm, sunny eli
mate and very early spring and
produces fruits, Strawberries and
other small garden truck in advance
of other sections of the northwest.
The soil, volcanic ash, has been
proven rich in the chemical con-
stituents required for the produc-
ton of alfalfa, fruits and vege-
tables.


The city of Kennewick has a
strategical location. Lying at the
confluence of the Columbia, Snake
and Yakima rivers, it not only en-
joys shipping facilities by water
north, east and west. but also the
Northern Pacific railway to Puget
Sound and the east; the Spokane,
Portland & Seattle railway to Port
land and Spokane, where it con-
meets with the Great Northern; the
North Coast railway (now build
ing), and the Kennewick Northern
(projected). With such varied
transportation facilities, added to
perfect climate and soil, it is agreed
by the far-seeing men of today that
Kenewick is destined to become the
metropolis of irrigated Washington.
Recognizing this fact, the leg.sla-
ture has located the permanent
office of the state commissioner of
horticulture at Kennewick.


WILL SUPPORT 100000


PEOPLE.


Thomas Cooper, land commission
er of the Northern Pacific Railway
Co.. is quoted as saying that this
district in 25 years will support
population of 100000 people. To
the farmer who is unfamiliar with
an irrigated district this statement
seems more or less exaggerated,
